summary: During exercise, cardiac output increases by increasing both heart rate and stroke volume.
During exercise, cardiac output increases only by increasing heart rate; stroke volume remains constant.
During exercise, cardiac output decreases due to increased peripheral resistance.
The body maintains core temperature at approximately 37 degrees Celsius through the hypothalamic thermoregulatory centre.
Core body temperature is maintained at 39 degrees Celsius by the cerebral cortex.
Body temperature is regulated solely by peripheral thermoreceptors in the skin.
High altitude acclimatisation involves increased erythropoietin production leading to polycythaemia.
High altitude acclimatisation involves decreased erythropoietin and anaemia.
At high altitude, the body compensates by reducing respiratory rate.
In starvation, the brain adapts to use ketone bodies as fuel when glucose is scarce.
The brain can only use glucose and cannot adapt to any other fuel source.
In starvation, the brain shifts to using fatty acids directly as fuel.
Fever is caused by pyrogens that raise the hypothalamic set point, triggering shivering and vasoconstriction.
Fever is caused by pyrogens that lower the hypothalamic set point, causing vasodilation.
Fever occurs when the hypothalamic set point is unchanged but heat production increases uncontrollably.
During exercise, blood flow to skeletal muscles increases through local metabolite-mediated vasodilation.
During exercise, blood flow to all organs increases equally due to increased cardiac output.
During exercise, blood flow to skeletal muscles decreases due to sympathetic vasoconstriction.

overallFeedback.feedback: Good effort! Review the areas you missed.
